title: Town For Sale

jokes.text: We're getting out! We're finally getting out!
jokes.setup: Season-long premise of the Rose family being trapped in Schitt's Creek
jokes.punchline: Moira's over-the-top operatic joy at the prospect of leaving is so disproportionate it becomes comic
jokes.explanation: Works because it's completely in character for Moira to respond to good news with theatrical excess. The repetition and escalation ('We're finally getting out!') mirror her dramatic persona. Solid but not extraordinary.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I've told this story forty-seven times.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Forty-eight.

jokes.text: Told you I'd sell this town. Didn't I tell you? / Yes. Not to diminish your accomplishment, but you did say that a handful of times. / I think eight, to be exact. / It bears repeating.
jokes.setup: Johnny triumphantly asks if he said he'd sell the town, expecting simple validation
jokes.punchline: Roland precisely counts the number of times Johnny said it (eight), and Johnny deflects with 'It bears repeating' — the pomposity is the joke
jokes.explanation: Tight three-beat exchange. Roland's pedantic exactitude punctures Johnny's self-congratulation, and 'It bears repeating' is a perfect Johnny Rose response — dignified bluster. Good ensemble comedy writing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Suitcatches.

jokes.text: My little suity-cases!
jokes.setup: Moira spots their luggage and exclaims with affection
jokes.punchline: She affectionately calls her suitcases 'suity-cases' — a portmanteau that reveals how deeply she anthropomorphizes luxury goods
jokes.explanation: Brilliant little moment. 'Suity-cases' is perfectly Moira — it's a character-specific word that only she would coin, combining twee affection with status-signaling. The economy is exceptional: two words tell you everything about this woman. Character integration is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You should take a coat. You look like you're about to commit a crime, and I'd prefer the police have as little to go on as possible.

jokes.text: David? Should I take this with me? / If I'm being perfectly honest, it's very low on the totem pole of coats of yours that I like.
jokes.setup: Moira asks David whether she should pack a particular coat
jokes.punchline: David's response implies he has extensively catalogued and ranked all of Moira's coats — the casualness of this elaborate opinion is the joke
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'totem pole of coats' does a lot of heavy lifting — it reveals David's fashion obsession and his relationship with his mother in one breath. Very much a David line; no other character could deliver it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This coat is a Valentino. Do you know how exclusive this is? I outbid Elon Musk for this coat at auction.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you did not outbid Elon Musk for a coat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, he wasn't there. But I would have.

jokes.text: I outbid Richard Branson for it at the Kaminski auction. I did get sick of having hot coffee and blood thrown at me.
jokes.setup: Moira defends the coat's pedigree by name-dropping an auction and a celebrity she outbid
jokes.punchline: She casually mentions 'blood' being thrown at her as an equal inconvenience to hot coffee — the dark non-sequitur delivered with total composure
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional joke. The structure is perfect: the Richard Branson name-drop sets up a register of glamorous celebrity anecdote, then 'blood' arrives completely unannounced, equated with hot coffee. Moira doesn't explain it, doesn't react to it — it just exists. The restraint is what makes it brilliant. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific. Justifies the 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm giving my coat to Jocelyn. She needs it far more than I do.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's very generous of you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the poor woman has been wearing the same three cardigans since we met her. I doubt she even owns a proper coat.

jokes.text: You know who covets this coat? Jocelyn. I could bestow it upon her as a parting gift.
jokes.setup: Moira is looking for a reason to get rid of the coat David doesn't like
jokes.punchline: She frames giving away an unwanted coat as a grand act of benevolence — 'bestow' and 'covets' reveal her self-perception as magnanimous royalty
jokes.explanation: The word choice ('bestow,' 'covets') is doing the work here — Moira can't help but frame everything as a royal dispensation. Functional character comedy but not as sharp as her other lines in this scene.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, those aren't your friends. Those are just people who knew you when you had money.

jokes.text: We may want to put pen to paper before we start giving away designer clothing, sweetheart.
jokes.setup: Moira announces she's going to give away a designer coat before the sale is finalized
jokes.punchline: Johnny's measured 'put pen to paper' is the voice of anxious reason against Moira's impulsive extravagance — the contrast is the joke
jokes.explanation: Solid Johnny Rose straight-man moment. The 'sweetheart' at the end is a perfect touch of condescension-disguised-as-affection. Functional but not standout.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, these people aren't your friends. They abandoned you the moment things got difficult.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not fair. They didn't abandon me. They simply chose not to maintain contact during a transitional period in my life.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Which, when you think about it, is a very reasonable response to someone losing their entire fortune and moving to a small town.

jokes.text: It would be her first piece of clothing made outside of mainland China.
jokes.setup: Moira is arguing that giving Jocelyn the coat is a generous act
jokes.punchline: She inadvertently reveals her classist condescension toward Jocelyn — framing a designer coat as an act of cultural uplift
jokes.explanation: Moira's oblivious snobbery is always funniest when she thinks she's being kind. 'Mainland China' as a marker of Jocelyn's economic status is both specific and biting. The line works on character-comedy and dark-subversive levels simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I'm not moving to Brooklyn to sit in a coffee shop and watch someone make a pour-over while I pretend to care about their sustainable sourcing practices.

jokes.text: I thought everyone just kind of dropped you like flies when you couldn't pick up the tab at the Waverly anymore?
jokes.setup: David claims he has friends in New York he's excited to reconnect with
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually recalls that all his friends abandoned him when the money ran out — delivered with sisterly cheerfulness as if it's common knowledge
jokes.explanation: The mixed idiom 'dropped you like flies' (instead of 'dropped like flies' or 'dropped you like a hot potato') feels intentional for Alexis — her casually garbled idioms are a character signature. The specificity of 'the Waverly' grounds it. Dark and funny because she's describing her brother's social humiliation with zero malice.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just think it's time for me to get my own place, you know? I need my independence.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you can barely take care of yourself. You're like a circus freak.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, David, that is so mean.

jokes.text: I just have a different kind of relationship with my friends where we don't need to be in constant contact with each other.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just implied David's friends abandoned him
jokes.punchline: David's rationalisation that his friends never contacting him is actually a sign of their special bond — transparent self-delusion delivered with absolute conviction
jokes.explanation: Classic defensive rationalisation joke. Works well because David sells it with total sincerity. The joke type (irony/character) is clear and well-executed, though not deeply original.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is a vintage Halston. I wore it to a benefit in the '80s.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It's beautiful. What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a coat, Jocelyn.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: No, I mean... what animal is it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ostrich.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh! Because I was going to say it looked like someone plucked a very large, very angry bird.

jokes.text: And do what, eat a cronut at a Brooklyn flea market?
jokes.setup: David suggests Alexis should come to New York after her vacation
jokes.punchline: Alexis dismisses the entire borough of Brooklyn with a single specific image — the cronut at a flea market as shorthand for a lifestyle she finds beneath her
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — 'cronut at a Brooklyn flea market' is a perfectly chosen image that does enormous work. It's specific enough to feel real, trendy enough to date-stamp the Rose family's cultural reference points, and entirely Alexis. She's not wrong, which makes it funnier. One of the best lines of the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You were absolutely green with envy over this coat.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I have no memory of that.

jokes.text: I was thinking, maybe we could get a two-bedroom apartment in Manhattan and not have to live in Brooklyn.
jokes.setup: David offers to share a New York apartment with Alexis, responding to her Brooklyn dismissal
jokes.punchline: He immediately concedes her Brooklyn objection and pivots to Manhattan — revealing he's already thought this through and is now negotiating
jokes.explanation: Works as a quick callback and reveals David's vulnerability — he wants his sister with him and is willing to negotiate. The comedy is subtle; more charming than funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, darling, this gift is a token of the profound bond we've shared. Our friendship has been... *pauses dramatically* ...transformative. You've been like a sister to me. A sister I barely tolerated, but a sister nonetheless.

jokes.text: Honestly, David, and, like, no offense, but when I get out of here, I'm gonna get my own place so I can pack how I wanna pack and I don't have to be called a circus freak all the time.
jokes.setup: David has proposed they share an apartment; Alexis is considering declining
jokes.punchline: 'I don't have to be called a circus freak all the time' — the specific grievance 'circus freak' implies David calls her this regularly enough that it's a motivating factor in her housing decisions
jokes.explanation: 'Circus freak' is the specific, unexpected detail that elevates this from a generic sibling complaint into a Schitt's Creek joke. It implies an entire off-screen history of David's commentary on Alexis's eccentric habits. The 'no offense' setup is a classic Alexis move — she says offensive things while inoculating herself.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You should really consider an all-inclusive vacation. You know, where everything is included?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what do you mean 'everything'? Like, the flights?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes, the flights, the hotel, the meals, the drinks, all the activities...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that sounds exhausting. Why would I want everything included? I like choosing things.

jokes.text: Ding-dong. Moira, hey! / Hi, Jocelyn. / Gosh, you look so glamorous today. / Oh, thank you. Some days it just works.
jokes.setup: Moira arrives at Jocelyn's door with the coat
jokes.punchline: Moira accepts the compliment with regal modesty — 'Some days it just works' — as if glamour is an unpredictable gift that occasionally descends upon her
jokes.explanation: 'Some days it just works' is a lovely, understated Moira moment — she's humble-bragging while implying that her glamour is a natural phenomenon beyond her control. Character-specific and warm.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, will you marry me?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, yes! I mean... if you were someone else, then yes.

jokes.text: Dear God, it feels good to be saying that! [referring to 'we're leaving']
jokes.setup: Moira interrupts Jocelyn to announce they're leaving
jokes.punchline: She immediately breaks off to savor the words 'we're leaving' aloud — the interruption and the self-indulgent aside is quintessential Moira
jokes.explanation: A moment of pure Moira — she can't even complete a social interaction without stopping to perform her own emotions. Functional and warm without being a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Just do what Carrie Bradshaw would do.

jokes.text: Wow. Is it a wig?
jokes.setup: Moira presents the coat as a grand gift
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn, unable to identify what she's being handed, guesses 'wig' — a perfectly plausible guess given Moira's theatrical wardrobe
jokes.explanation: Two words, enormous payoff. The joke works on multiple levels: it's a visual gag (the coat apparently looks ambiguous enough to be mistaken for a wig), it's a character joke about Moira's known wig collection, and it deflates Moira's grand gesture perfectly. The economy is nearly perfect — 'Is it a wig?' says everything. Justifies 8+ score: it's specific, surprising, and deeply character-integrated in a way that couldn't exist in another show.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I actually need an office. I have a very demanding job that requires a separate workspace.

jokes.text: It's the coat. / What coat? / The coat you kept going on about that one time. / Are you sure it was me?
jokes.setup: Moira insists this is THE coat Jocelyn always wanted
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn has no memory of coveting this coat whatsoever — 'Are you sure it was me?' — exposing Moira's self-serving false memory
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff works beautifully — Moira invented the coat-coveting narrative entirely to justify giving away something she didn't want. Jocelyn's confusion is the perfect straight-man response. 'Are you sure it was me?' is quietly devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for the excellent service, Twyla. You've been wonderful.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, thank you, Mr. Rose. Though I have to say, your tip is about as generous as your compliments are frequent.

jokes.text: Oh, it's absolutely tragic, Jocelyn, that I will never see or speak to you ever again. But I will have my memories and you will have this incredible coat.
jokes.setup: Moira is delivering what she frames as a heartfelt farewell
jokes.punchline: She says it's 'absolutely tragic' she'll never see Jocelyn again with zero apparent sadness, then equates their friendship with the coat — a garment she didn't even want
jokes.explanation: One of the best Moira-Jocelyn moments. She declares their friendship 'absolutely tragic' to lose while demonstrating through the coat exchange that she values Jocelyn roughly at the price of an unwanted designer garment. The cruelty is entirely unintentional, which makes it funnier and warmer simultaneously — classic Schitt's Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, look, I gotta tell you something, and I'm not gonna sugarcoat it. It's like when you got a dead cat in your pool — you can't just leave it there hoping it'll float away. Eventually somebody's gotta jump in and fish it out.

jokes.text: Originally, we were gonna be on an all-inclusive vacation when I did this. / What's an all-inclusive vacation?
jokes.setup: Ted is nervously explaining his proposal context, acknowledging the setting isn't ideal
jokes.punchline: Alexis has no idea what an all-inclusive vacation is — a concept entirely alien to someone who has only ever vacationed on private islands and yachts
jokes.explanation: Elegant in its simplicity. Ted's nervous admission that a resort vacation was his aspirational proposal setting is already slightly deflating, and then Alexis's genuine bafflement at the concept of an all-inclusive — a middle-class staple — recontextualizes the entire gap between their worlds without a word of explanation. The pause before Alexis asks the question is where the laugh lives. Justifies near-8 score.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The speakerphone during sex is worse than the sex call itself

jokes.text: Alexis Claire Rose, will you marry me? / Um... [long pause] / Yes! / If I was staying, then yes! A thousand times yes! / And if things were different and I wasn't leaving, um, then definitely yes. / So, is that a no? / Yes.
jokes.setup: Ted proposes to Alexis in an emotional moment
jokes.punchline: Alexis's answer is a cascade of enthusiastic 'yes's that are all conditional on her not leaving — culminating in Ted asking 'So, is that a no?' and Alexis saying 'Yes' (ambiguously confirming the no)
jokes.explanation: This is the comedic centerpiece of the episode. The structure is near-perfect: the proposal setup earns genuine emotion, then Alexis's conditional 'yes' is funny, then 'A thousand times yes!' amplifies it, then the 'if things were different' clause further undercuts it, and the whole thing lands on Ted's devastated 'So, is that a no?' followed by Alexis's confirming 'Yes' — which is simultaneously grammatically correct, emotionally chaotic, and perfectly Alexis. The cringe is earned, not cheap. One of the show's signature jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's inappropriate behavior. It's like when Johnny does that thing where he just stands there looking all confused and useless.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm right here, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Exactly my point.

jokes.text: Are you stealing Roland's truck? / Yeah. It's not exactly a Tesla, but it'll get our bags to the airport.
jokes.setup: David spots Johnny doing something with Roland's truck
jokes.punchline: Johnny confirms he's stealing it, then defends his theft on practical grounds while acknowledging the vehicle's inadequacy compared to his previous standards
jokes.explanation: Johnny casually admitting to theft while comparing Roland's truck unfavorably to a Tesla is a nice moment of his residual entitlement. The 'not exactly a Tesla' grounds the joke in character history without being too on-the-nose.

dialogue.speaker: Buyer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm looking for a douchebag named Johnny Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you've found him.

jokes.text: Oh, you just watch a season of Girls and do the opposite of what they do. It's easy.
jokes.setup: Patrick says he doesn't know what he would do in New York
jokes.punchline: David's advice is to use HBO's Girls as a guide for what NOT to do — both a sharp cultural reference and a self-revelatory statement about David's own New York self-image
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. The Girls reference is perfect for David's cultural frame of reference and his snobbishness about New York authenticity. It's also a genuinely useful piece of observational comedy that lands independently of knowing the show. 'It's easy' sells it perfectly — David's breezy confidence that navigating New York is simply a matter of aesthetic opposition to bad TV. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I'm going back to my natural habitat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, are you like, a penguin or something?

jokes.text: I was looking at three bedrooms, but do I really need an office? I don't know.
jokes.setup: David is selling Patrick on the two-bedroom apartment
jokes.punchline: He's already been apartment-hunting and debating the need for a home office — the casual implication that he's gone this far without Patrick agreeing reveals his presumption
jokes.explanation: Reveals David's endearing presumption — he's been planning Patrick's life without consultation. The 'I don't know' sells his genuine uncertainty about whether he merits an office. Good character work, mild comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, it's actually really attractive how you can just... let me go like that. Most guys would at least pretend to care.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I do care.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, you have a really weird way of showing it.

jokes.text: I'm saving you from this dumpy town.
jokes.setup: David is trying to convince Patrick to come to New York, accelerating into enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: Framing his invitation as a rescue mission — 'saving you' — reveals David's self-perception as a cosmopolitan savior
jokes.explanation: Functional character moment. 'Dumpy town' has a slight callback energy to the season's premise. Mild.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it sounds like you two really didn't see eye to eye.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I suppose the deal fell through because he was looking for something a bit more... beneath you?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Or perhaps he simply didn't measure up to your expectations.

jokes.text: Yeah, judging by your tips, I was never really sure.
jokes.setup: Johnny thanks Twyla for great service over the past months
jokes.punchline: Twyla, with perfect cheerfulness, mentions that his terrible tips made his appreciation unclear — a gentle, devastating rebuke
jokes.explanation: An almost perfect one-line joke. Twyla's warmth and simplicity make her the ideal delivery vehicle for a passive-aggressive observation she doesn't even realize is passive-aggressive. The structure is impeccable: Johnny's earnest thanks sets up the expectation of reciprocal warmth, and Twyla pivots to economic receipts. 'I was never really sure' is the ideal deadpan kicker. Justifies near-8 score.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: This is a real designer coat? Jocelyn gave me this? There's no way this is real.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It is real, Twyla.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, it can't be. You don't have that kind of money.

jokes.text: I don't wanna piss in your punch bowl, okay, but, um, I got a bad feeling about this buyer guy of yours.
jokes.setup: Roland approaches Johnny to share a concern
jokes.punchline: 'Piss in your punch bowl' as Roland's chosen idiom — coarser than needed, entirely Roland
jokes.explanation: Roland's idiom choice is always slightly more vulgar than the situation requires. Functional character moment for Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I'm so excited to wear this coat in winter. It's so glamorous.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: It reminds me of the coats the women wear at the bingo hall.

jokes.text: He called me while he was bumping uglies with his old lady. / Oh, no, really? / But that's not the problem. The problem is he put me on the speakerphone.
jokes.setup: Roland reveals Andy called during sex — establishing what seems like the problem
jokes.punchline: The sex call is NOT the problem — speakerphone is the real issue, revealing Roland's bizarre but internally consistent moral hierarchy
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection structure. The setup leads us to believe the sex is the transgression; Roland's pivot to speakerphone as the actual moral offense is a perfect subversion. Roland's logic is both absurd and internally coherent — he's fine being called during sex, but speakerphone is a violation of personal dignity. The joke earns its 8 through structural precision and character-specific absurdist logic.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I feel so glamorous in this coat. I feel like Patty LaBelle.

jokes.text: I mean, that's something you would do. / No, no. That's not something I would do. / And I can't deal with another you.
jokes.setup: Roland says speakerphone is something 'you would do,' and Johnny denies it
jokes.punchline: 'I can't deal with another you' — Roland's greatest fear is encountering someone as irritating as Johnny
jokes.explanation: Good Roland-Johnny dynamic. The irony that Roland — who is objectively MORE irritating than Johnny — finds Johnny intolerable is a nice layer. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Well, Mutt's been doing exemplary work. Really top-notch performance.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's great!

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I think it has more to do with the fact that you've been working here.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I just... I like being around you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: And here's the kicker, the second one was a hooker, too!
jokes.setup: Roland is telling a story about the buyer; the line seems to be a non-sequitur since we haven't heard 'the first one'
jokes.punchline: We've missed the context — Roland announces 'the second one was a hooker, too' mid-conversation as if this detail is the kicker, but we have no idea what came before it
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is structural — we arrive mid-story to hear only the most explosive detail. Roland delivers it with no fanfare as if it's a logical conclusion. The 'too' implies a first hooker we never heard about, which is funnier than explaining it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not going to New York because I don't want to watch you fall in love with someone else.

jokes.text: Excuse me, honey, do you know where I can find Johnny Rose? I got a meeting with this freakin' douche.
jokes.setup: Andy asks a bystander where he can find Johnny Rose
jokes.punchline: Johnny is standing right there — 'The douchebag is right here' — making Andy's casual insult a face-to-face humiliation
jokes.explanation: A classic face-reveal joke — someone insulting the person they're looking for to that same person. The execution is clean and the timing works. 'The douchebag is right here' as Johnny's response is slightly elevating — he's both offended and pragmatic about it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie's been a great friend.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My only friend, really.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, not my only friend. I have other friends. Plenty of them. All over the place.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'The douchebag is right here.'
jokes.setup: Andy has just called Johnny a 'freakin' douche' to his face without knowing who he's talking to.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's completely flat, unruffled self-identification as 'the douchebag' is the punchline — he absorbs the insult with perfect equanimity and turns it into an introduction.
jokes.explanation: Five words, maximum impact. The economy here is exceptional — Johnny doesn't get angry, doesn't correct, just adopts the epithet as his introduction. It's the ideal payoff to the setup established seconds earlier.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You have lots of friends who'd want to live with you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I have you. And I consider you a friend. So that's one. And then there's my mother, who I'm forced to see regularly. So that's two people who have to tolerate me because of legal or emotional obligation. And then there's Patrick, but he works for me, so I'm not sure that counts. So really, I have no friends.

jokes.text: So, you're leaving. / What'd Ted say about that? / He proposed. / Really? / So, what'd you tell him? / I told him I was leaving. / But if you weren't, you would? / Is this a Barbara Walters special or something? I'm leaving. That's it.
jokes.setup: Mutt is asking Alexis pointed questions about Ted and her feelings
jokes.punchline: 'Is this a Barbara Walters special or something?' — Alexis deflects genuine emotional inquiry with a celebrity TV reference, refusing to engage
jokes.explanation: The Barbara Walters reference is specific and character-appropriate — Alexis would know Barbara Walters as the queen of uncomfortable celebrity interviews. Using it as a deflection reveals her emotional avoidance. The 'That's it' is a good kicker.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh wow, 'survivable.' That's so romantic. I'm going to have that embroidered on a pillow.

jokes.text: You don't seem to be crying about it. / I think it's nice you're going back... / I'm not a dolphin, Mutt.
jokes.setup: Mutt starts to say something that sounds like 'it's nice you're going back to your natural habitat'
jokes.punchline: Alexis cuts him off at 'going back' and snaps 'I'm not a dolphin' — a pre-emptive non-sequitur that is both absurd and reveals her sensitivity about being compared to wildlife
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. Four words — 'I'm not a dolphin' — do extraordinary work. It's absurdist (why would Mutt be implying she's a dolphin?), character-specific (only Alexis would leap to this conclusion), and lands with perfect economy. The joke also implies a history — presumably someone has compared her to a dolphin before, or she's simply sensitive about animal comparisons in a very specific way. Rewatch value is enormous because you can never quite believe it happened. Justifies 8.5+ score handily.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's like sex in a pan.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not sure I'd know what that tastes like.

jokes.text: You know what I meant. / I'm happy for you. / Oh. / I'm glad you're happy about it. / You know, you should really work on expanding your emotional range at some point.
jokes.setup: Mutt says he's happy Alexis is going back to her world
jokes.punchline: Alexis tells Mutt he should 'expand his emotional range' — clearly wanting him to express sadness, she's disguising her disappointment as life coaching
jokes.explanation: Alexis telling a stoic man to 'expand his emotional range' when she is the emotionally repressed one is a good irony. The line works as both comedy and character revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Did you ever wear that coat I gave you?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I gave it to my sister right away.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You re-gifted my coat?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: It wasn't really my style.

jokes.text: So, what you're saying is he got a little short with you? / [beat] / You, uh, weren't quite seeing eye to eye?
jokes.setup: Johnny is discussing a conflict involving Andy (the short buyer) with Roland
jokes.punchline: Johnny delivers two puns about Andy's height ('short with you,' 'seeing eye to eye') in rapid succession with deadpan delivery, seemingly not realizing he's making puns
jokes.explanation: The double pun structure is competent — the second one lands harder because it confirms the pattern is intentional (or that Johnny is obliviously doing it twice). The beat between them is the comic timing. Functional wordplay, not spectacular.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: One simply does not wear white after Labor Day, one does not return a gift, and one does not use the word 'moist' in polite company. It's just not done.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And frankly, neither is microwaving fish in a shared office space, or asking someone how much they paid for their home, or wearing Crocs to the theatre.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Absolutely forbidden. Simply barbaric.

jokes.text: So, are we gonna sign this puppy or what?
jokes.setup: Context of a serious business transaction
jokes.punchline: Andy's casual 'this puppy' to describe a $975,000 town sale — reveals his bro-ish financial nonchalance
jokes.explanation: Minor character-establishing moment for Andy. 'Sign this puppy' as language for a million-dollar deal is mild color comedy. Not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So why are you really staying in Schitt's Creek?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I just want to stay active.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You literally sit in a barn all day.

jokes.text: Well, excuse you in that coat! / I know! Jocelyn gave it to me. / What? / Knockoffs are getting so good these days.
jokes.setup: Alexis sees Ronnie wearing the designer coat that Moira gave Jocelyn who then gave to Ronnie
jokes.punchline: Ronnie assumes it's a knockoff — correctly identifying it as expensive-looking while getting the situation entirely backward
jokes.explanation: The coat has now passed through three hands in one episode. 'Knockoffs are getting so good these days' is a great punchline — Ronnie admiringly assumes it's fake because she can't conceive of owning a real one. The callback payoff is well-structured and the episode has earned it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mutt, you need to explain to me right now why you have extra community service.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Alexis, I... it's complicated.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Complicated? We're supposed to be together and you can't even talk to me about this?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I know, and I'm sorry. I just... I don't know how to say this.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, you're going to have to figure it out because I deserve an explanation.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You're right. You do. I just... I can't seem to find the words.

jokes.text: Thanks. I can't wait till winter. I'm gonna feel like Patty LaBelle.
jokes.setup: Ronnie is wearing the fancy coat and seems pleased
jokes.punchline: Her aspiration in this coat is to feel like Patty LaBelle — a specific, charming, unexpected reference that says a lot about Ronnie
jokes.explanation: Two-sentence character portrait. 'Patty LaBelle' is exactly the right celebrity for Ronnie to invoke — it's deeply specific, unexpected, and tells you everything about her. The economy is excellent. A small gem.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, I need to talk to you about the money from the town sale.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, not now. I'm trying to figure out if this fanny pack is coming back in style.

jokes.text: And you must have done some number on Mutt. / What do you mean? / His hours were up a month ago, he kept coming anyway.
jokes.setup: Ronnie compliments Alexis on her work, then hints at something else
jokes.punchline: Mutt's community service ended a month ago but he kept showing up — revealed as a fact that Alexis didn't know
jokes.explanation: The laugh comes from Alexis's reaction — she doesn't know, and the revelation recontextualizes Mutt's presence all season. More of a dramatic beat with a comedic wrapper, but the comedy of Alexis being oblivious to this obvious sign is real.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was just thinking, you know, with the right attitude and a little bit of flexibility, we could really make this thing work between us.

jokes.text: Maybe he just likes pretty girls in hippie hats. / Know what I mean, string bean?
jokes.setup: Ronnie is suggesting Mutt stayed for Alexis's company
jokes.punchline: 'String bean' as Ronnie's affectionate nickname for Alexis — delivered casually, simultaneously warm and absurd
jokes.explanation: 'String bean' is a perfect Ronnie moment — the nickname is unexpected, affectionate, and slightly diminishing, exactly how Ronnie would operate. The 'hippie hats' observation about Alexis is also gently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Okay, so I have the contract ready for you to sign. Now, the numbers look really good. We've got some beautiful female numbers here, and then some strong, masculine numbers over here.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: But honestly, I'm more of a numbers person than a sales person, so I'm just going to let the numbers do the talking.

jokes.text: I like you. I don't want to like you, but I do.
jokes.setup: Patrick is declining David's invitation but has to explain why sharing a space won't work
jokes.punchline: The admission 'I don't want to like you, but I do' is simultaneously a confession and a mild insult — it acknowledges David's somewhat exhausting nature while still expressing genuine feeling
jokes.explanation: In retrospect (given the show's arc), this line becomes enormously loaded. As a standalone joke, 'I don't want to like you, but I do' is a perfectly economical confession-as-backhanded-compliment. It's funny because of its honesty — he's actively resisting liking David and failing. Rewatch value is very high.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, the buyer just collapsed. I think he's having a heart attack.

jokes.text: You've made my life here survivable. / 'Survivable.' Thank you. That is the nicest thing anyone has ever said to me.
jokes.setup: David is trying to express gratitude to Patrick for their friendship
jokes.punchline: David chooses 'survivable' as his highest compliment, and Patrick's 'That is the nicest thing anyone has ever said to me' is so deadpan it's impossible to tell if he means it — and the ambiguity is the joke
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best. The joke works on every level: David's best compliment is 'survivable' (reveals his own low regard for Schitt's Creek while accidentally revealing how much this friendship matters), and Patrick's response is either sincere (he genuinely has been told nothing nicer) or devastatingly dry. The ambiguity is perfect. Economy is exceptional — both beats are short and land hard. Justifies 8+ comfortably.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is he okay? Should we call an ambulance?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Ambulance? Are you crazy? We're in the middle of closing a deal here. Andy, buddy, just sign right here. Your hand can do it. Come on.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, the man is unconscious.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: An unconscious signature is still a signature, John. It's legally binding. I looked it up.

jokes.text: Well, 'bursting' seems to be the operative word. [referring to the mac and cheese]
jokes.setup: Andy said the town is 'bursting with opportunity'; they're now at the table eating and apparently the food is extraordinary
jokes.punchline: Johnny pivots the word 'bursting' from business enthusiasm to food enthusiasm — a gentle callback that connects the two registers
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay that earns a mild smile. The callback works but isn't inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, the buyer seemed very interested. I think this could actually work out.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I'm going to go home, take some pills, and hope I slip into a coma.

jokes.text: Oh. Oh. / Slow down there, pal. We got dinner coming and a big dessert after. / Oh. Oh. / Sex in a pan. / Then we'll have dessert.
jokes.setup: Roland says they have 'a big dessert after,' implying something sweet
jokes.punchline: Andy says 'Sex in a pan' — which is apparently the name of the dessert — and Roland's 'Then we'll have dessert' implies Andy has misunderstood or the line is a double entendre
jokes.explanation: 'Sex in a pan' as a real dessert name — layered pudding — creates genuine ambiguity about whether Andy knows it's a dessert or is propositioning someone. Roland's 'Then we'll have dessert' implies Andy may have meant the other thing. Double entendre well-deployed.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm not sure what my relationship status is at this point. Like, am I dating him? Are we just... existing in the same space? I honestly don't know.

jokes.text: So, did you try it on? / What? / The coat. The one I gave you. / Yes. It's great. / Not too snug? / Nope. Buttoned right up. / It zips. / Zips. It zipped right up!
jokes.setup: Moira casually asks if Jocelyn tried on the coat
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn claims it 'buttoned right up' — but Moira knows it zips — so Jocelyn's overly enthusiastic 'It zipped right up!' reveals she never actually wore it
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed cringe sequence. Moira's single word — 'It zips' — is the trap, and Jocelyn walks right into it, over-correcting with 'It zipped right up!' The micro-escalation between 'buttoned' and 'zips' is perfectly observed. The audience is always a step ahead of Jocelyn, which is cringe comedy at its finest.

jokes.text: I gave it away. / I'm sorry. / It just... It didn't suit me, and I thought about Ronnie and you know she could always use some more feminine pieces in her wardrobe.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn confesses she gave away the coat Moira just discovered she never tried on
jokes.punchline: Her justification — Ronnie 'could always use some more feminine pieces' — is an accidentally cutting commentary on Ronnie's presentation, delivered with Jocelyn's characteristic oblivious warmth
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's well-meaning shade at Ronnie is perfectly in character — she thinks she's doing Ronnie a favor, unaware of how the comment reads. The coat saga continues to generate comedy.

jokes.text: Well, Jocelyn, there are certain things that are just not done. Smoking in a car with a baby, unless you crack a window, tipping before tax, mixing drinks with cola, and giving away a coat that doesn't belong to you.
jokes.setup: Moira is reprimanding Jocelyn for giving away the coat
jokes.punchline: Moira's list of social taboos escalates from actually harmful (smoking near a baby) through mildly eccentric (tipping before tax) to absurd (mixing drinks with cola) and lands on giving away someone else's coat — equating all of them as equal moral failings
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of Schitt's Creek's finest moments. The list structure is masterfully deployed: starting with something genuinely bad (smoking near a baby) but immediately qualifying it ('unless you crack a window') collapses Moira's moral seriousness. The progression through increasingly petty concerns, all delivered with identical gravity, is perfect escalation. 'Mixing drinks with cola' is inspired — it's specific, tells you everything about her class values, and is completely indefensible as a moral position. The kicker (the coat) is the point of it all but barely the worst thing on the list. Near-perfect joke. Justifies 8.8 craft score easily.

jokes.text: Sure. / Don't you even care that I'm leaving? / Of course I care. / Then what're you doing? / Why are you yelling? / Okay, I'm gonna ask you one more time. Mutt, why did you do that extra community service? / You know why! / Why can't you just say it?
jokes.setup: Alexis pushes Mutt to admit he stayed for her; he won't say it
jokes.punchline: Mutt finally explodes with 'You know why!' — confirming everything without actually saying it — and Alexis's exasperated 'Why can't you just say it?' is the final beat of two people unable to communicate directly
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the frustration — Mutt and Alexis are both equally bad at expressing feelings, but in opposite directions. He won't say it; she demands he say it. The 'You know why!' is an emotionally honest non-answer that the scene earns. More cringe than comedy but the cringe is real.

jokes.text: David, what're you doing here? / I'm just wondering when you think we're gonna have the check ready. / Well, this is not the time for that, David.
jokes.setup: Johnny is in the middle of a critical business dinner trying to close a million-dollar deal
jokes.punchline: David shows up to demand his share of the money — with zero awareness of timing or context
jokes.explanation: Classic David — his personal priorities completely override any situational awareness. The comedy is in his absolute confidence that NOW is the right moment to discuss money.

jokes.text: Okay, well, you gave me this town as a gift, so I feel like I'm owed a little bit more. So, 50% for me, 50% for you.
jokes.setup: David is arguing he deserves half the sale price of the town
jokes.punchline: His logic: the town was given to him as a gift (technically true), therefore he owns half. The math is both wrong and stated with complete conviction.
jokes.explanation: David's entitlement logic is always funny when it's technically defensible but obviously wrong. The 50/50 split proposal delivered at a dinner party is peak David obliviousness.

jokes.text: Talk to the hand, son, because I've gotta watch a pig eat.
jokes.setup: David keeps pressing for his money; Johnny needs him to leave
jokes.punchline: 'Talk to the hand' is already a dated dismissal, but 'because I've gotta watch a pig eat' is the unexpected, specific reason that elevates it entirely
jokes.explanation: 'Talk to the hand, son, because I've gotta watch a pig eat' is a great line. The 'Talk to the hand' sets up an old-fashioned dismissal, then the reason — watching a pig eat — is specific and bizarre enough to be genuinely funny. Johnny delivering it with complete seriousness as a legitimate priority is perfect character comedy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Is it just me, or is it this dessert that's making me horny?
jokes.setup: Andy is enthusiastically eating Jocelyn's mac and cheese at a business dinner
jokes.punchline: He asks — of the entire table including his hosts — whether the dessert is causing his arousal
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the social transgression — this is a deeply inappropriate thing to say at a business dinner. Works as character comedy for Andy (a crude man) and as cringe comedy for the table. 'I think it's just you, Andy' as the straight-man response is perfect.

jokes.text: I think it's just you, Andy.
jokes.setup: Andy asks if the dessert is 'making him horny'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's measured, polite correction — 'I think it's just you' — delivered with the resigned dignity of a man who really needs this sale
jokes.explanation: Six words, perfectly delivered. Johnny's ability to manage Andy's awfulness while maintaining professional decorum is the running joke of this dinner scene. The economy is close to perfect.

jokes.text: You know what? I think I should let you guys do the nasty. And then sign the contract.
jokes.setup: The dinner is going well; Roland is in the spirit of things
jokes.punchline: Roland casually suggests Andy sleep with Jocelyn as a closing incentive, then adds the contract as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: Roland being Roland — his moral compass points in a completely different direction. The 'and then sign the contract' ordering (sex first, business second) is his priority list made visible. Dark but characteristic.

jokes.text: $975,000! Wow. That's a heck of a lot of cheddar! Don't you think so, sweet cheeks? / 'Sweet cheeks'? / Aren't you the old-fashioned charmer.
jokes.setup: Andy sees the sale price and turns to Moira
jokes.punchline: Moira's response to being called 'sweet cheeks' — 'Aren't you the old-fashioned charmer' — is so arch and withering it functions as both a compliment and an evisceration
jokes.explanation: Moira's ability to receive a vulgar compliment and return something that sounds polite but is entirely devastating is peak character comedy for her. 'Old-fashioned charmer' is beautifully double-edged.

jokes.text: You know, Andy, $975,000 suddenly seems like such a girly number. What are we doing, honey? I mean a million. That's bold, that's sexy.
jokes.setup: Johnny has put his signature on the $975,000 contract and is about to have Andy sign
jokes.punchline: Moira, entirely unprompted, changes the sale price mid-signing — calling $975,000 'a girly number' and reframing the round number as more masculine and sexy
jokes.explanation: Moira's negotiation instinct is both impressive and absurd. 'Such a girly number' is a wonderfully strange piece of logic — she's gendering a number to manipulate a man. It actually works, which makes it funnier. Johnny's silent horror is the implied straight-man reaction.

jokes.text: That's quite the little lady you have there. / You know, I think the little lady's right. What's an extra $25,000, Andy? Come on, it's chump change. It's cab fare.
jokes.setup: Andy reacts to Moira's price renegotiation
jokes.punchline: Johnny, utterly panicked a moment ago, instantly pivots to echoing Moira's language — 'chump change,' 'cab fare' — to close the deal, his salesman instincts overcoming his shock
jokes.explanation: Johnny's instinctive pivot from horror to salesmanship is a nice character moment. The escalation from 'chump change' to 'cab fare' is a good two-beat close.

jokes.text: Sign on... What's happening? / What? Oh, my God! Andy! Andy! / We had him, we had him. / Okay, put the pen — / Ah! / Sign! He was going to sign. / Andy? / He wanted to sign, Johnny. / That's it, that's it. / Just on the line. / What is happening here? / What? What happened? Oh, no! Andy! What happened? / Circulation, Johnny! Circulation in his hand!
jokes.setup: Andy is about to sign the million-dollar contract
jokes.punchline: Andy has some kind of medical episode right as the pen touches paper — apparently circulation issues in his signing hand — and the entire sale collapses in chaos, with everyone panicking in different ways
jokes.explanation: Excellent farce sequence. The comedy is in the chaos and the characters' completely divergent reactions — Moira trying to force the pen, Roland bellowing 'Circulation!', Johnny in absolute despair. Each character stays true to themselves even in crisis, which is good sitcom writing. 'Circulation, Johnny!' is Roland's finest hour.

jokes.text: People do come out of comas. / Oh, I'd kill for a good coma right now.
jokes.setup: Johnny (apparently hopefully) notes that people do come out of comas
jokes.punchline: Moira responds with the revelation that she would love to be in a coma right now — implying her current situation is worse than unconsciousness
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's very best. The setup is Johnny's characteristic optimism reaching into dark territory (he appears to be noting that Andy might recover). Moira's response — 'I'd kill for a good coma right now' — reframes the coma as aspirational rather than feared, which is a perfect dark subversion. The 'good coma' is the brilliant modifier — as if comas come in quality grades. Entirely Moira; no other character on television could say this line. Justifies 8.5 comfortably.

jokes.text: I'm pretty sure I'm engaged, and I'm pretty sure I just cheated on my fiancé.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if Alexis will be all right
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually delivers two enormous revelations with the qualifier 'pretty sure' attached to both — she doesn't know if she's engaged (she said yes-but-no) and she apparently cheated on someone while all of this was happening
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best and a great season-finale button. The double 'pretty sure' is the structural genius — she's uncertain about both her engagement status AND whether she cheated, implying she either wasn't sure if Ted's proposal counted or isn't sure what she did with Mutt constitutes cheating. The casual delivery of catastrophic personal news is quintessential Alexis. Highly quotable, great rewatch value. Justifies 8.4 craft.

jokes.text: She'll be okay. / Where's your brother? / Who? / David.
jokes.setup: Johnny reassures Moira about Alexis; Moira asks about David
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'Who?' in response to 'Where's your brother?' — a beat implying he's so exhausted by the evening that he's momentarily forgotten David exists, or that David is so peripheral to him that the word 'brother' doesn't immediately conjure an image
jokes.explanation: A small final gag — Johnny's 'Who?' at the mention of 'your brother' is a good end-of-episode beat that implies the evening has broken him. Works as a mild closing joke.

tmdb_overview: Johnny finds a potential buyer for the town, while David and Alexis discover they may have become more attached to Schitt’s Creek than they ever thought possible.
insight: Season finale lands 82 with character-driven cringe as Rose family hits rock bottom together.
